domain_intelligence.description: I chose biology-specific content_words (autophagy, mTOR, AMPK, ubiquitin, p62, LC3, lysosome) and then ran biology-flavored queries. Predictable consequence: biology source-domain papers dominated the result set with 17 forced hits, even though many are not LLM prior art in the spirit of the saturation check. This is a queries-bias issue, not a verdict-changing one (the AI-autophagy agent-judged hits suffice independently for FAIL), but the cleaner protocol would be to use one query family that biases LLM-side and one that biases AI-side, never the pure-biology source domain.

honest_note: Two things felt wrong: (1) Half my forced hits are pure biology papers on autophagy mechanism. The rule mechanically hits these. I had a strong impulse to override and exclude all biology-source results — I did not, in line with the program's explicit instruction that the rule wins. The verdict (FAIL) is still robust because of the AI-autophagy agent-judged hits (Model Autophagy Disorder, AI autophagy Nature MI paper, autophagy-and-ML paper, constructive/destructive autophagy in AI blog). (2) The candidate is FAIL on the broader analogy, not on the specific selective-autophagy-as-typed-cleanup variant. The 'AI autophagy' term means model collapse via synthetic data, which is conceptually different from selective-autophagy as memory architecture. A different round could try to defend the narrower position — but per the saturation_evidence pattern, even narrowing tends to find an adjacent published frame. Not pursuing the narrowing here.
